meta.title: SANAD Breakthrough-Seizure Outcomes
meta.authors: Bonnett LJ, Powell GA, Tudur Smith C, et al.
meta.outcome: Breakthrough seizure, subsequent seizure recurrence, and re-remission after a period of 12-month remission (SANAD)
meta.clinical_utility: The SANAD trial followed 2,627 people with newly-diagnosed epilepsy. Of those who achieved a first 12-month remission on antiepileptic treatment, this analysis (Bonnett et al., PLOS ONE 2017) modelled three outcomes and reported each as an overall Kaplan–Meier curve — there is NO risk score or points system in the paper. Curve 1 (Fig 2): risk of a first breakthrough seizure after achieving remission — rising to ~58% by ~5.6 years. Curve 2 (Fig 3): among people who have a breakthrough seizure, the risk of a further seizure recurrence — a steep rise to a ~76% ceiling. Curve 3 (Fig 4): among those people, the chance of re-achieving another 12-month remission — flat until 12 months then rising to ~82%. Use the outcome selector to switch curves and the time slider to read the probability at any month. The paper also reported clinical factors (e.g. neurological insult, more prior tonic-clonic seizures, longer time to first remission) that raised breakthrough-seizure risk, but these came from separate models and are not combined into a single calculator here.

model.selector_info: SANAD-BT reports three separate Kaplan–Meier curves, each a different outcome after a person achieves a first 12-month remission on treatment. Choose which curve to display: (Fig 2) the risk of a first breakthrough seizure; (Fig 3) among those who have a breakthrough, the risk of a further seizure recurrence; (Fig 4) among those who have a breakthrough, the chance of re-achieving 12-month remission. Each is an overall curve — the paper reports no patient-level score or points.

breakthrough.info: Fig 2 — starts the clock when a patient first reaches 12 months seizure-free on treatment. Rises to ~24% at 1yr, ~37% at 2yr, ~58% by ~5.6yr. In the SANAD cohort, 34% of participants went on to have a first breakthrough seizure.

recurrence.display: Risk of seizure after a breakthrough (Fig 3)
recurrence.info: Fig 3 — the clock starts at the first breakthrough seizure. Rises steeply: ~68% at 1yr, ~74% at 2yr, plateauing at a ~76% ceiling. Of those who had a breakthrough, 63% went on to a further seizure recurrence.

remission.info: Fig 4 — a favourable outcome: the chance of re-achieving another 12-month remission after a breakthrough seizure. Flat at 0 until 12 months (remission requires 12 seizure-free months by definition), then rises to ~29% at 1yr past that point, ~64% at 2yr, ~82% ceiling. Overall, 44% re-achieved remission. COSY is not shown for this curve — it is not a seizure-risk outcome.

provenance.source_paper: PMID28698335_2017_Risk of a seizure recurrence after a breakthrough seizu.pdf
provenance.digitizer: riskcalc auto-trace (contiguous-run axis detection + topmost-dark trace)
provenance.notes: Values digitised from the paper's own Kaplan–Meier curves (Figs 2–4); the paper reports no score/points. Curve ceilings from the digitised traces: breakthrough-seizure risk rises to ~58% by ~5.6yr (24% at 1yr, 37% at 2yr); seizure-recurrence-after-breakthrough rises to a ~76% ceiling (68% at 1yr, 74% at 2yr); chance of re-achieving 12-month remission is flat until 12mo then rises to ~82% (29% at 1yr, 64% at 2yr). Separately, the paper's TEXT quotes flow-through fractions of the SANAD cohort (34% proceed to a first breakthrough, 63% of those go on to recurrence, 44% re-achieve remission) — these are cohort proportions, NOT the curve ceilings. Human review of the three overlays required before deploy.
